div.h2: Formal Boundary: What the Lexer Can and Cannot Recognize

p.em: The boundary is not arbitrary — it is the halting point of regular automata.

div.p: The Halting Theorem for Lexers When you want to check balanced braces in the lexer — stop. That belongs in the parser. The boundary is not arbitrary; it is the mathematical halting point of regular automata. A DFA has finite state — it cannot count to arbitrary depth. The moment a construct requires remembering how many of something you have seen (nested parens, open braces, template brackets), you have crossed into Context-Free territory and need a stack. Pumping Lemma consequence: No regular expression can match { ... } at arbitrary nesting depth. This is why lexers never check brace balance — it is provably impossible with finite state. Nesting block comments (/* /* */ */) would require a counter → context-free → cannot be a lexer rule. C chose non-nesting /* */ precisely to keep comment scanning regular.
Requires symbol tables & multi-pass analysis Cannot be expressed as a grammar production

text.tspan: `x = 1` vs `y = x` — is x in scope?

div.p: Requires a push-down automaton (stack) Needs to count/match arbitrarily nested structure
Recognized by a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) Single forward pass, O(1) state, no stack required
DFA properties: no recursion · no stack · single forward pass · O(n) time · 1-char lookahead max
